source_text: I think Maria talks too much.

reference_translations.text: Penso che Maria parli troppo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. penso che triggers the present subjunctive; parlare singular subjunctive is parli.

reference_translations.text: Credo che Maria parli troppo.
reference_translations.notes: credo che is an equivalent opinion trigger.

explanation: After an opinion verb (penso che, credo che) Italian uses the present subjunctive in the che-clause. parlare takes -i in the singular: parli, not the indicative parla.

common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative parla after penso che. Guards the indicative-instead-of-subjunctive miss.

source_text: It's important that you all work harder.

reference_translations.text: È importante che voi lavoriate di più.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. è importante che triggers the subjunctive; the voi -are subjunctive is -iate: lavoriate.

reference_translations.text: È importante che lavoriate di più.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun voi dropped; the verb ending already marks the person.

explanation: The voi present subjunctive is -iate for every conjugation: lavoriate, distinct from the indicative lavorate. The impersonal è importante che requires the subjunctive.

source_text: I hope they leave early tomorrow.

reference_translations.text: Spero che partano presto domani.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. partire takes -ano in the 3rd-person plural subjunctive: partano, not the indicative partono.

reference_translations.text: Spero che se ne vadano presto domani.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with andarsene; valid but shifts the verb. The intended target is partano.

explanation: spero che triggers the subjunctive. The -ere/-ire 3rd-person plural subjunctive is -ano: partano. The indicative partono is the common slip.

source_text: I doubt the shop opens on Sundays.

reference_translations.text: Dubito che il negozio apra la domenica.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. dubito che triggers the subjunctive; aprire is a plain -ire verb, so the 3sg is apra.

reference_translations.text: Dubito che il negozio sia aperto la domenica.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative using essere aperto (to be open) rather than the verb aprire; both natural.

explanation: dubito che (doubt) requires the subjunctive. aprire is a plain -ire verb with no -isc-, so the 3rd-person singular subjunctive is apra, not the indicative apre.

source_text: I don't think he understands the problem.

reference_translations.text: Non credo che capisca il problema.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, subject pronoun dropped. capire is an -isc- verb: capisca in the singular subjunctive.

reference_translations.text: Non credo che lui capisca il problema.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun lui included for emphasis or clarity.

explanation: capire belongs to the -isc- group, which inserts -isc- in the singular: capisca. non credo che (negated opinion) strongly favours the subjunctive.

common_errors.text: Non credo che capisce il problema.
common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative capisce after non credo che. Guards the indicative miss.

source_text: It's necessary that you all finish the work today.

reference_translations.text: Bisogna che voi finiate il lavoro oggi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The voi subjunctive of an -isc- verb drops the -isc-: finiate, not finisciate.

reference_translations.text: Bisogna che finiate il lavoro oggi.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun voi dropped.

explanation: The -isc- insert appears in the singular and 3rd-person plural but never in noi/voi. The voi subjunctive of finire is finiate; finisciate (carrying the insert in) is the over-extension to avoid.

source_text: I want you to look for a solution.

reference_translations.text: Voglio che tu cerchi una soluzione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. cercare (-care) inserts an h before the subjunctive -i: cerchi.

reference_translations.text: Voglio che cerchi una soluzione.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun tu dropped; the cerchi ending marks the person.

translation.reveal: to look for / to search - cercare

explanation: English 'want someone to do' becomes voglio che + subjunctive in Italian. cercare ends in -care, so it inserts an h to keep the hard c before the -i ending: cerchi.

source_text: It's only right that they pay the bill.

reference_translations.text: È giusto che paghino il conto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. pagare (-gare) inserts an h: paghino in the 3rd-person plural subjunctive.

reference_translations.text: È giusto che siano loro a pagare il conto.
reference_translations.notes: Cleft alternative emphasising 'they'; valid but restructures. The intended target is paghino.

explanation: è giusto che is an impersonal evaluation trigger requiring the subjunctive. pagare ends in -gare, so it adds an h before the -ino ending: paghino, not pagano (indicative) or pagino (missing h).

source_text: I hope Marco comes to the party tonight.

reference_translations.text: Spero che Marco venga alla festa stasera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. venire builds its subjunctive from vengo: venga.

reference_translations.text: Spero che Marco venga alla festa questa sera.
reference_translations.notes: questa sera for stasera; equivalent.

explanation: This irregular group builds the present subjunctive from the io indicative: vengo gives venga. spero che requires the subjunctive, ruling out the indicative viene.

common_errors.text: Spero che Marco viene alla festa stasera.
common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative viene after spero che. Guards the indicative miss.

source_text: I think they are all leaving tonight.

reference_translations.text: Penso che vadano via tutti stasera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. andare builds its subjunctive from vado: 3rd-person plural vadano.

reference_translations.text: Penso che se ne vadano tutti stasera.
reference_translations.notes: With andarsene; natural and idiomatic.

translation.reveal: to leave / to go away - andare via

explanation: andare builds the subjunctive from vado: the 3rd-person plural is vadano, not the indicative vanno. penso che triggers the subjunctive.

source_text: I want you to pay attention in class.

reference_translations.text: Voglio che tu faccia attenzione in classe.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. fare builds its subjunctive from faccio: faccia.

reference_translations.text: Voglio che faccia attenzione in classe.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun tu dropped.

explanation: 'pay attention' is fare attenzione in Italian. fare builds the subjunctive from faccio: faccia. voglio che requires the subjunctive.

source_text: I think it is a good idea.

reference_translations.text: Penso che sia una buona idea.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. essere has the suppletive subjunctive sia.

reference_translations.text: Credo che sia una buona idea.
reference_translations.notes: credo che, an equivalent trigger.

explanation: essere has its own subjunctive, sia, not derivable from any other form. After penso che it replaces the indicative è. This is the highest-frequency subjunctive form to master.

common_errors.text: Penso che è una buona idea.
common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative è after penso che. Guards the very common essere indicative miss.

source_text: I don't believe he is right.

reference_translations.text: Non credo che abbia ragione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, subject pronoun dropped. avere ragione = to be right; subjunctive of avere is abbia.

reference_translations.text: Non credo che lui abbia ragione.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun lui included.

explanation: Italian says avere ragione (to have reason) for 'to be right'. avere has the suppletive subjunctive abbia, with a double b and no h. non credo che favours the subjunctive over the indicative ha.

source_text: I hope your father is feeling better now.

reference_translations.text: Spero che tuo padre stia meglio adesso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'to feel (well/unwell)' is stare; the subjunctive of stare is stia.

reference_translations.text: Spero che tuo padre stia meglio ora.
reference_translations.notes: ora for adesso; equivalent.

explanation: Italian uses stare for physical/health states: sta meglio = is feeling better. The subjunctive of stare is stia; spero che requires it over the indicative sta.

source_text: I thought he talked too much.

reference_translations.text: Pensavo che parlasse troppo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, subject pronoun dropped. A past-tense trigger (pensavo che) selects the imperfect subjunctive: parlasse.

reference_translations.text: Pensavo che lui parlasse troppo.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun lui included.

explanation: A past-tense opinion verb pulls the che-clause into the imperfect subjunctive, not the present: pensavo che parlasse. The -are imperfect subjunctive is the stem plus -asse in the 3rd singular.

common_errors.note: WRONG: imperfect indicative parlava after pensavo che. Guards the indicative-instead-of-subjunctive miss.

source_text: I wanted you to make a decision.

reference_translations.text: Volevo che tu prendessi una decisione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. volevo che (past verb of will) selects the imperfect subjunctive; prendere gives prendessi.

reference_translations.text: Volevo che prendessi una decisione.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun tu dropped.

translation.reveal: to make (a decision) / to take - prendere

explanation: English 'wanted you to do' becomes volevo che + imperfect subjunctive. Each conjugation keeps its class vowel: -ere gives -essi, so prendessi (not prendassi).

source_text: If I had more time, I would read more.

reference_translations.text: Se avessi più tempo, leggerei di più.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The unreal if-clause takes the imperfect subjunctive (avessi) and the main clause the present conditional (leggerei).

reference_translations.text: Se io avessi più tempo, leggerei di più.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun io included.

explanation: In an unreal 'if' sentence Italian puts the imperfect subjunctive in the if-clause and the conditional in the main clause: se avessi ..., leggerei. Never se avrei. Avere is regular here: av- + -essi.

common_errors.text: Se avrei più tempo, leggerei di più.
common_errors.note: WRONG: conditional in the if-clause. The signature 'se avrei' error; the if-clause must take the imperfect subjunctive.

source_text: I hoped they were already home.

reference_translations.text: Speravo che fossero già a casa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. essere has the irregular imperfect subjunctive fossero.

reference_translations.text: Speravo che loro fossero già a casa.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun loro included.

explanation: Essere is the only irregular imperfect subjunctive: fossi, fossi, fosse, fossimo, foste, fossero. It is not derivable from era; it is learned outright.

common_errors.text: Speravo che erano già a casa.
common_errors.note: WRONG: imperfect indicative erano after speravo che. Guards the indicative miss.

source_text: I didn't think she did it on purpose.

reference_translations.text: Non pensavo che lo facesse apposta.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. fare restores the fac- stem: facesse.

reference_translations.text: Non pensavo che lei lo facesse apposta.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun lei included.

explanation: Fare expands to the longer fac- stem, the same one behind the imperfect indicative facevo: fac- + -esse gives facesse. If you know facevo, you know facesse.

source_text: I hoped your father was feeling better.

reference_translations.text: Speravo che tuo padre stesse meglio.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. stare contracts to stes-: stesse. Italian uses stare for how someone feels.

reference_translations.text: Speravo che tuo padre stesse meglio adesso.
reference_translations.notes: With adesso; equivalent.

explanation: Italian uses stare for physical and health states. Its imperfect subjunctive contracts to stes-: stesse, not the regularised stasse. Speravo che (past trigger) selects the imperfect subjunctive over the indicative stava.

source_text: I think Marco has already spoken to her.

reference_translations.text: Penso che Marco le abbia già parlato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Present trigger + past action = compound past subjunctive; auxiliary in the present subjunctive (abbia).

reference_translations.text: Penso che Marco abbia già parlato con lei.
reference_translations.notes: con lei instead of the indirect clitic le; both natural.

explanation: The compound past subjunctive is the PRESENT subjunctive of the auxiliary plus the participle: abbia parlato. After penso che the auxiliary cannot stay indicative (ha parlato).

common_errors.text: Penso che Marco ha già parlato con lei.
common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative auxiliary ha. The defining compound miss.

source_text: I think the girls arrived on time.

reference_translations.text: Penso che le ragazze siano arrivate in orario.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. arrivare takes essere: auxiliary siano (present subjunctive), participle agrees feminine plural (arrivate).

reference_translations.text: Penso che le ragazze siano arrivate puntuali.
reference_translations.notes: puntuali for in orario; equivalent.

explanation: Two things at once: the essere auxiliary goes into the present subjunctive (siano), and with essere the participle agrees with the subject (le ragazze, feminine plural: arrivate).

common_errors.text: Penso che le ragazze sono arrivate in orario.
common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative auxiliary sono after penso che.

source_text: I'm sorry that I couldn't help you yesterday.

reference_translations.text: Mi dispiace che io non abbia potuto aiutarti ieri.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Emotion trigger + past action = abbia potuto.

reference_translations.text: Mi dispiace di non averti potuto aiutare ieri.
reference_translations.notes: Same-subject infinitive alternative, very natural, but sidesteps the subjunctive. The intended target is abbia potuto.

explanation: An emotion trigger (mi dispiace che) takes the subjunctive; because the action is past relative to a present main clause, it is the compound past subjunctive: abbia potuto.

source_text: If I had known earlier, I would have come.

reference_translations.text: Se avessi saputo prima, sarei venuto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Impossible past condition: trapassato subjunctive in the if-clause, past conditional in the main clause.

reference_translations.text: Se lo avessi saputo prima, sarei venuto.
reference_translations.notes: With the object clitic lo; very natural.

explanation: An impossible past condition puts the trapassato subjunctive in the if-clause: the IMPERFECT subjunctive of the auxiliary plus the participle (avessi saputo). The conditional never goes in the if-clause.

common_errors.text: Se avrei saputo prima, sarei venuto.
common_errors.note: WRONG: conditional in the if-clause. The signature 'se avrei' error.

source_text: I thought Maria had gone out with her friends.

reference_translations.text: Credevo che Maria fosse uscita con gli amici.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. uscire takes essere: fosse (imperfect subjunctive) + uscita (agrees, feminine singular).

reference_translations.text: Pensavo che Maria fosse uscita con le sue amiche.
reference_translations.notes: pensavo for credevo, le sue amiche for gli amici; equivalent.

translation.reveal: to go out / to leave - uscire

explanation: A past main clause plus an earlier action gives the trapassato subjunctive: the imperfect subjunctive of the auxiliary (fosse) plus the participle, which agrees with Maria under essere: uscita.

common_errors.text: Credevo che Maria era uscita con gli amici.
common_errors.note: WRONG: imperfect indicative auxiliary era. The defining trapassato miss.

source_text: I didn't know they had finished the work.

reference_translations.text: Non sapevo che avessero finito il lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, subject pronoun dropped. avere auxiliary in the imperfect subjunctive: avessero.

reference_translations.text: Non sapevo che loro avessero finito il lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun loro included.

explanation: The auxiliary takes the imperfect subjunctive (avessero), not the imperfect indicative (avevano). With avere the participle stays invariable: finito.

source_text: Let him do it himself, then!

reference_translations.text: Che lo faccia lui, allora!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Independent che + present subjunctive for a third-person command; fare gives faccia.

reference_translations.text: Che se la sbrighi da solo, allora!
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic alternative (sbrigarsela); still the exhortative subjunctive.

explanation: Italian has no third-person imperative, so 'let him do it' is che + the present subjunctive: Che lo faccia. The indicative fa is wrong here.

common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative fa in the exhortative. The defining miss.

source_text: Let them make the decision.

reference_translations.text: Che prendano loro la decisione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Third-person plural exhortative subjunctive: prendano.

reference_translations.text: Che decidano loro.
reference_translations.notes: With decidere; equally natural.

translation.reveal: to make (a decision) / to take - prendere

explanation: The exhortative extends to the plural: che + third-person plural subjunctive, prendano. loro after the verb marks the subject as focus.

source_text: If only it were true!

reference_translations.text: Magari fosse vero!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Magari + imperfect subjunctive for an out-of-reach wish; essere gives fosse.

reference_translations.text: Se solo fosse vero!
reference_translations.notes: Se solo, an equivalent optative opener.

explanation: A wish about something not real takes Magari + the imperfect subjunctive: Magari fosse vero. With the indicative (Magari è vero) the sense flips to 'maybe it's true'.

common_errors.note: WRONG: indicative è. Magari with the indicative means 'maybe', not a wish; the wish needs fosse.

source_text: If only I had more free time!

reference_translations.text: Magari avessi più tempo libero!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Present unreachable wish: imperfect subjunctive avessi.

reference_translations.text: Se solo avessi più tempo libero!
reference_translations.notes: Se solo variant.

explanation: A present, out-of-reach wish takes the imperfect subjunctive: Magari avessi. Not the indicative ho or the conditional avrei.

source_text: If only you had listened to me!

reference_translations.text: Magari mi avessi ascoltato!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. A PAST regret takes the trapassato subjunctive: avessi ascoltato.

reference_translations.text: Se solo mi avessi ascoltato!
reference_translations.notes: Se solo variant.

explanation: A regret about the past takes the trapassato subjunctive: Magari avessi ascoltato (imperfect subjunctive of avere + participle). The present wish would be the imperfect subjunctive; the past one is the compound.

common_errors.note: WRONG: conditional avresti. A past wish/regret takes the trapassato subjunctive, not the conditional.

source_text: As far as I know, it's closed on Mondays.

reference_translations.text: Che io sappia, è chiuso il lunedì.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Frozen hedging formula che io sappia + subjunctive sappia.

reference_translations.text: Per quanto ne sappia, è chiuso il lunedì.
reference_translations.notes: Per quanto ne sappia, an equivalent frozen hedge, also subjunctive.

explanation: 'As far as I know' is the frozen che io sappia, always with the subjunctive sappia. The main clause stays indicative (è chiuso).

source_text: We'll finish it, cost what it may.

reference_translations.text: Lo finiremo, costi quel che costi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Frozen concessive costi quel che costi, subjunctive both times.

reference_translations.text: Lo finiremo, a qualunque costo.
reference_translations.notes: Non-subjunctive paraphrase (a qualunque costo); loses the target formula.

explanation: 'Cost what it may' is the frozen costi quel che costi, the subjunctive costi wrapped around quel che. The indicative costa breaks the formula.
